op.name: linalg.eigvals
op.summary: Compute the eigenvalues of a general matrix.

blocks.text: Compute the eigenvalues of a general matrix.

Main difference between `eigvals` and `eig`: the eigenvectors aren't
returned.

desc_blocks.text: A complex- or real-valued matrix whose eigenvalues will be computed.

The eigenvalues, each repeated according to its multiplicity.
They are not necessarily ordered, nor are they necessarily
real for real matrices.

blocks.text:
Broadcasting rules apply, see the `numpy.linalg` documentation for
details.

This is implemented using the ``_geev`` LAPACK routines which compute
the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of general square arrays.

Illustration, using the fact that the eigenvalues of a diagonal matrix
are its diagonal elements, that multiplying a matrix on the left
by an orthogonal matrix, `Q`, and on the right by `Q.T` (the transpose
of `Q`), preserves the eigenvalues of the "middle" matrix. In other words,
if `Q` is orthogonal, then ``Q * A * Q.T`` has the same eigenvalues as
``A``:

blocks.code:
>>> import numpy as np
>>> from numpy import linalg as LA
>>> x = np.random.random()
>>> Q = np.array([[np.cos(x), -np.sin(x)], [np.sin(x), np.cos(x)]])
>>> LA.norm(Q[0, :]), LA.norm(Q[1, :]), np.dot(Q[0, :],Q[1, :])
(1.0, 1.0, 0.0)

blocks.text:
Now multiply a diagonal matrix by ``Q`` on one side and
by ``Q.T`` on the other:

blocks.code:
>>> D = np.diag((-1,1))
>>> LA.eigvals(D)
array([-1.,  1.])
>>> A = np.dot(Q, D)
>>> A = np.dot(A, Q.T)
>>> LA.eigvals(A)
array([ 1., -1.]) # random

blocks.text: eig — eigenvalues and right eigenvectors of general arrays
eigvalsh — eigenvalues of real symmetric or complex Hermitian (conjugate symmetric) arrays.
eigh — eigenvalues and eigenvectors of real symmetric or complex Hermitian (conjugate symmetric) arrays.
